EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed across the county including PA 849/Duncannon Subway between US 22/322 and Market Street in Penn Township; Bistline Bridge Road between Red Rock Road and Couchtown Road in Southwest Township; PA 850 between PA 274/Shermans Valley Road and Center Road in Northeast Madison Township; PA 850 between PA 34/Spring Road in Carroll Township and Mill Road in Spring Township; Fowlers Hollow Road between Union Road/Back Hollow Road in Toboyne Township and Manassas Road in Jackson Township; Dellville Road between Linton Hill Road in Wheatfield Township and Haas Drive in Penn Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time within a week heavy rain fell across Eastern Pennsylvania. An additional 1.5 to 5 inches of precipitation fell and caused more widespread river, stream and poor drainage flooding including the Delaware, Lehigh and Schuylkill Rivers. On average moderate flooding occurred on the Lehigh River, with several locations recording top ten crests. Generally minor flooding occurred on the Delaware and Schuylkill Rivers. Snowmelt in the upper reaches of the Lehigh and Delaware Basins contributed ti the flooding. The worst reported damage was along the towpaths of the Lehigh River in Northampton County and the Delaware River in Bucks County. The heaviest precipitation amounts occurred in the Poconos, while the lightest precipitation amounts occurred in and around Philadelphia. Precipitation started during the late evening of the 9th, fell heavy at times during the daylight hours and evening on the 10th and ended between 3 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 11th. | |The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Southern Plains on the morning of the 8th northeast into Iowa on the morning of the 9th, Michigan on the morning of the 10th and into the eastern Great Lakes by the morning of the 11th. This system was able to tap moisture from both the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean and lift it over its frontal boundaries. The deep southeast flow was able to enhance the precipitation further over the higher terrain of Eastern Pennsylvania. The rain ended once the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flooding Lehigh River destroyed fencing in Weissport. Sections of the Lehigh Canal Towpath were washed away and closed. In Lehighton, flooding along the Mahoning Creek covered walking paths in the Bear Creek Memorial Park. The Lehigh River at Lehighton had moderate flooding and was above its 10 foot flood stage from 1050 p.m. EST on the 10th through 1042 a.m. EST on the 11th. It crested at 12.95 feet at 345 a.m. EST on the 11th. This was the 8th highest crest on record. The Pohopoco Creek at Kresgeville (Monroe County) was above its 8.5 foot flood stage from 341 a.m. EST through 813 a.m. EST on the 11th. It crested at 8.77 feet at 615 a.m. EST. The Tobyhanna Creek at Blakeslee (Monroe County) had major flooding and was above its 10 foot flood stage from 1221 a.m. EST through 1100 p.m. EST on the 11th. It crested at 12.04 feet at 1000 a.m. EST on the 11th. The Aquashicola Creek at Palmerton was above its 7 foot flood stage from 458 a.m. EST through 101 p.m. EST on the 11th. It crested at 7.59 feet at 830 a.m. EST.||Event precipitation totals included 4.25 inches in Jim Thorpe, 4.22 inches in Bowmanstown, 4.13 inches in Coalsdale, 3.89 inches in Weatherly and 3.86 inches in Lehighton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the central United States and into|the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of February 21st. Snow associated|with a warm frontal boundary stretched across the Ohio Valley into the Mid-Atlantic|moved into northeast Pennsylvania late on the evening of February 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 5 to 9 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front pushed northward across northeast Pennsylvania late in the afternoon on April 25th. A warm and relatively moist air mass covered the region, with showers and storms developing, especially in the unstable air south of the frontal boundary, where breaks in the cloud cover allowed temperatures to rise to near 70 degrees. Severe weather developed, and in addition to reports of severe wind damage and hail, areas of heavy rain caused significant flash flooding in several locations of northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along Route 6 in Plymouth Township, with scattered areas of road flooding elsewhere across Luzerne county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several bands of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along two north-south axes of low level confluence across central Pennsylvania into upstate New York. The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and intense short-duration rainfall from the mid afternoon hours through the late evening. The combination of the heavy rain, above normal soil moisture and high stream-flows resulted in significant localized flash flooding in Sullivan County which lasted into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood brought widespread damage to the town of Dushore.  Numerous roads were closed.  Several homes were damaged due to high water.  Two water rescues were performed in Cherry and Colley Townships.  German Street Bridge, SR 220 was damaged from flood waters.  About 30 residents were displaced due to flooding in downtown Dushore.  Two feet of water was estimated in downtown Dushore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania and mainly minor to moderate creek and  river flooding in Berks County and Southeastern Pennsylvania. The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches with the highest amounts along the interstate 95 corridor and the Poconos.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Manatawny Creek at Spangville was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1048 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 518 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 6.72 feet at 130 a.m. EDT on the 17th.  Storm precipitation totals included 2.41 inches in Bechtelsville, 2.08 inches in Hamburg, 1.85 inches in Fleetwood, 1.79 inches in Berne, 1.69 inches in Bethel, 1.67 inches in Wyomissing, 1.61 inches in West Lawn, 1.47 inches in Reading and 1.26 inches in Oley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across central and eastern Pennsylvania. The storms produced widespread wind damage and several reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damaging microburst /straight line wind damage/ affected an area approximately 3 miles east of Muncy near the intersection of PA 405 and 442. The peak winds associated with the microburst were estimated at 90 mph, which snapped or uprooted several trees and produced minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked east across the northern Great Lakes during the morning and afternoon hours. The trailing cold front entered northwest Pennsylvania late that afternoon bringing a line of thunderstorms and high winds through the area. With the ground already saturated from the above normal precipitation, flooding also occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous large trees, which downed several power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked east across the northern Great Lakes during the morning and afternoon hours. The trailing cold front entered northwest Pennsylvania late that afternoon bringing a line of thunderstorms and high winds through the area. With the ground already saturated from the above normal precipitation, flooding also occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several tree limbs and tore off shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong southeast wind up the Delaware Bay and River combined with the already high astronomical tides associated with the full moon and fresh water runoff from heavy rain to produce severe tidal flooding along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries during the overnight high tide cycle on the 16th.||In Bucks County, homes along Minot Street in Bristol Township suffered basement flooding after a clapper failed. In Philadelphia, Columbus Boulevard and Delaware Avenue were completely shut down between Spring Garden Street and Washington Avenue. Cars were flooded by several feet of water. The Interstate 95 off ramp to Columbus Boulevard was closed. The tidal flooding waters came close, but did not reach the Waterfront Square Condominiums or Sugarhouse Casino on Delaware Avenue. In Delaware County, tidal flooding reached the John Heinz National Wildlife Refuge in Tinicum Township.||The high tide at Philadelphia Pier 12 reached 10.49 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on the 17th.  Severe tidal flooding begins at 10.2 feet above mean lower low water. This tied the all-time record high tide set during the November 25th nor'easter in 1950. The high tide at Marcus Hook (Delaware County) reached 9.80 feet above mean lower low water.||The strong southeast winds persisted and reached its peak as the high tide was working its way up the Delaware Bay and Delaware River. The surface winds did not shift to the west until about one hour before the high tide occurred in Philadelphia. Minor tidal flooding also occurred during the overnight high tide cycles on the 17th, 18th and 19th, but not on the magnitude of the 16th.

A fast-moving upper level impulse and attendant surface low pressure system moved across the Great Lakes Region. An unseasonably warm and relatively moist air mass /surface temperatures in the 70s to low 80s and dewpoints in the upper 50s/ resided over central Pennsylvania in advance of this system and aided in strong destabilization. The moderate instability combined with strong vertical wind shear profiles promoted organized thunderstorms with supercells and bowing line segments. Large hail and damaging winds were the main threats, however sufficient low-level wind shear was present to support isolated tornadoes. A tornado watch was issued around 1500 EDT on 27 April 2011.||The severe weather threat continued through the evening and into the overnight and early morning hours on 28 April 2011. By this time, thunderstorms had evolved into largely north-south oriented clusters or broken lines with embedded supercell structures. Above normal soil moisture from recent heavy rains combined with high stream-flows and intense short duration rainfall contributed to flash flooding. The storms finally exited the region by mid-morning on 28 April 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 234 was closed due to high water.  Small stream flooding, water rescues, basements flooded and water flowing over blocking roads reported in Biglerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant severe weather and flash flood outbreak developed across northeast Pennsylvania as a storm system moved east from the Great Lakes.  Thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain developed and moved across the area during the overnight hours on the 28th. One supercell thunderstorm produced a tornado in Susquehanna county. Significant flash flooding also occurred across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near exit 201 off I-81 and State Route 438.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down. School was closed due to blocked roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front pushed northward across northeast Pennsylvania late in the afternoon on April 25th. A warm and relatively moist air mass covered the region, with showers and storms developing, especially in the unstable air south of the frontal boundary, where breaks in the cloud cover allowed temperatures to rise to near 70 degrees. Severe weather developed, and in addition to reports of severe wind damage and hail, areas of heavy rain caused significant flash flooding in several locations of northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along several roads in Wysox.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damage, and spawned two tornadoes during the afternoon of June 12th, 2011 in the Lower Susquehanna Region of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team and staff from the York County Emergency Management Agency concluded that an EF0 tornado, with peak winds around 75 mph,  touched down briefly during the afternoon of June 12th 2011. The tornado touched down at 1530 EDT about 1/4 mile northeast of Springvale, east of Red Lion. The tornado traveled to the southeast, and lifted just south of Bahnsmill Road at 1531 EDT. Several homes were affected, with one tree down on a house. A total of about 15 trees were damaged or downed. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania and mainly minor to moderate creek and  river flooding in Berks County and Southeastern Pennsylvania. The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches with the highest amounts along the interstate 95 corridor and the Poconos.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding along several creeks within Philadelphia. Moderate flooding occurred along the Pennypack and Frankford Creeks and minor flooding occurred along the Wissahickon Creek. Flooding along the Wissahickon Creek closed Lincoln Drive. The eastbound lanes of the Schuylkill Expressway were closed near Montgomery Drive because of a mudslide. Flooding also occurred in the city's Bridesburg section near the Frankford Creek. A man had to be rescued from the top of a vehicle at the intersection of Hadley and Richmond Streets. Tidal flooding also occurred along the Delaware River.||The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue was above its 7 foot flood stage from 902 p.m. EDT through 1027 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 9.84 feet at 930 p.m. EDT. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 929 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 620 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 10.18 feet at 1130 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth was above its 5 foot flood stage from 854 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 750 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 6.91 feet at 230 a.m. EDT on the 17th. Storm precipitation totals included 3.12 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 2.98 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 2.78 inches in Wyndmoor and 2.51 inches in Rockledge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic area of low pressure and trailing frontal zone moved across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening, producing intense lines of heavy showers and thunderstorms. Deep moisture and very strong wind fields ahead of the frontal system produced torrential rains, flash flooding and localized wind damage across the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in 12 water rescues of people in cars on flooded roads at several locations.  Cedar Run was reported out of banks and flooding Old Gettysburg Road near Shiremanstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic area of low pressure and trailing frontal zone moved across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening, producing intense lines of heavy showers and thunderstorms. Deep moisture and very strong wind fields ahead of the frontal system produced torrential rains, flash flooding and localized wind damage across the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous water rescues of stranded motorists were made by boats and helicopters. No injuries were reported but several people were taken to area hospitals for evaluation. Several residences were evacuated due to high waters along area creeks. The Gettysburg Fire Department, assisted by several local fire departments, evacuated 27 boy scouts via boat from the Gettysburg Campground. The boy scouts were relocated to the Eisenhower Inn. SR 94 was closed due to high water in Reading Township. Helicopters used in rescue operations were provided by Troop 3 of Maryland State Police and Stat Medivac from Thomasville, York County, PA. Opossum Creek was also out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell over the higher terrain of the Poconos from the late evening of March 30th through the afternoon of April 1st. A mixture of rain and then snow fell in the valleys. Elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain changed to snow during the morning of April 1st as the last surface low pressure system pulled away from the region. Snowfall ranged from an inch or two in Chester and Berks Counties and the Lehigh Valley to around six inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. Some less traveled and untreated roadways in the Poconos were slippery. In the Poconos, intermittent snow started on the evening of March 30th and became steady during the evening of the 31st. The heaviest snow fell during the morning commute on April 1st. The snow became lighter as the morning progressed and little additional snow was able to accumulate until it ended that afternoon. Aside from the higher terrain of the Poconos, the snow had a difficult time accumulating on non-grassy surfaces.  ||Representative snowfall included 6.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 4.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.3 inches in Kutztown (Berks County), 2.2 inches in Mohnton (Berks County), 2.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and Exton Chester (County), 1.9 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 1.5 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.4 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County) and 0.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County). ||The snow was caused by a pair of low pressure systems that formed over the southeastern United States. The first one reached the Delmarva coastal waters during the early evening of March 31st and weakened as the second low pressure system exited the Georgia coast. At 2 a.m. EDT on April 1st, the second low pressure system reached the North Carolina coastal waters and had become the stronger of the two lows. It continued to intensify and move northeast and at 8 a.m. EDT on April 1st, it reached Cape Cod Massachusetts.

Aside from the higher terrain of the Poconos, the snow had a difficult time accumulating on non-grassy surfaces.  ||Representative snowfall included 6.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 4.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.3 inches in Kutztown (Berks County), 2.2 inches in Mohnton (Berks County), 2.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and Exton Chester (County), 1.9 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 1.5 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.4 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County) and 0.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County). ||The snow was caused by a pair of low pressure systems that formed over the southeastern United States. The first one reached the Delmarva coastal waters during the early evening of March 31st and weakened as the second low pressure system exited the Georgia coast. At 2 a.m. EDT on April 1st, the second low pressure system reached the North Carolina coastal waters and had become the stronger of the two lows. It continued to intensify and move northeast and at 8 a.m. EDT on April 1st, it reached Cape Cod Massachusetts.

A dynamic area of low pressure and trailing frontal zone moved across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening.  Deep moisture and very strong wind fields ahead of the frontal system produced torrential rains with 3-4 inches of rain observed.  Several river points exceeded flood stage, with two points cresting above moderate and major flood stages respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aughwick Creek at Shirleysburg reached moderate flood stage.  Flood waters covered portions of Gilbo Road, Aughwick Mills Road and Keystone Road in Shirley Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania and mainly minor to moderate creek and  river flooding in Berks County and Southeastern Pennsylvania. The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches with the highest amounts along the interstate 95 corridor and the Poconos.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as minor flooding along the Brandywine, Chester and Crum Creeks in Delaware County. The Chester Creek in Chester was above its 8 foot flood stage from 937 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 1205 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 8.74 feet at 1030 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 804 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 1246 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 7.44 feet at 10 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 502 a.m. EDT through 1117 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 9.58 feet at 815 a.m. EDT on the 17th. Storm precipitation totals included 3.12 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 2.32 inches in Chadds Ford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front pushed northward across northeast Pennsylvania late in the afternoon on April 25th. A warm and relatively moist air mass covered the region, with showers and storms developing, especially in the unstable air south of the frontal boundary, where breaks in the cloud cover allowed temperatures to rise to near 70 degrees. Severe weather developed, and in addition to reports of severe wind damage and hail, areas of heavy rain caused significant flash flooding in several locations of northeast Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to high southeast winds affected Southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph with some isolated wind gusts to around 60 mph. The highest wind gusts occurred during the evening. The strong to high winds coupled with the heavy rain, knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires. The strong to high gradient winds were exacerbated further by isolated severe thunderstorms. About 25,000 homes and businesses lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania.  ||Peak wind gusts included 61 mph in Springtown (Bucks County), 58 mph in West Grove (Chester County) and 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and the Philadelphia International Airport. The strong to high winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system departing the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley. Winds eased after the occluded front moved through Pennsylvania late in the evening on the 16th.

An area of very strong low pressure moved northeast across the Great Lakes during the evening hours of April 27th and the morning hours of the 28th. A strong cold front stretching south from this low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania prior to daybreak on the 28th. Following the passage of the cold front, strong northwesterly winds developed over northwestern Pennsylvania. Peak winds at most locations occurred around daybreak on the 28th. Winds gusted to as much as 60 mph in some areas and some damage was reported. Most of the damage was from downed trees, power lines and utility poles. Some homes also sustained damage, mainly from lost roofing or siding.  Scattered power outages were reported with at least 3,100 homes and business affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed several trees across Crawford County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic frontal system moved across the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the late afternoon and evening, producing intense lines of heavy showers and thunderstorms. Very fast winds just off the surface combined with strong low-level vertical wind shear created a convective environment capable of producing a few weak tornadoes, particularly along the leading edge of narrow thunderstorm line segments. A weak, very brief EF0 tornado was confirmed near York during the late evening resulting in 2 minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS in State College along with York County Emergency Management officials confirmed a weak, brief, EF0 tornado touched-down in West Manchester Township a few miles west of York between 2020 and 2021 PM EDT. The EF0 tornado first produced damage just north of Brougher Lane where damage to trees, utility wires and several outbuildings occurred. Some minor damage to several homes also occurred in this area. The tornado damaged a canopy on a gas station and two billboards at the corner of Carlisle Road and Brougher Lane as it traveled northeast along a short 1/2 mile path. After crossing Carlisle Road, more tree damage was observed. A significant portion of a roof was removed off a business and blown across Brougher Lane near Haviland Road. The tornado briefly lifted but touched-down again at the West Manchester Mall. Several trees were uprooted and a large 40x40 foot skylight was lifted and blown across the roof of the mall. The damage indicators observed during the storm survey were consistent with a maximum wind speed of 85 mph, rating the tornado as a high-end EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several bands of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along two north-south axes of low level confluence across central Pennsylvania into upstate New York. The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and intense short-duration rainfall from the mid afternoon hours through the late evening. The combination of the heavy rain, above normal soil moisture and high stream-flows resulted in significant localized flash flooding in Sullivan County which lasted into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KJST ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 53 knots or 61 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bradford County experienced significant flash flooding on the overnight period of April 26th into April 27th.  The bulk of the damage was roadway related with most townships in the county reporting at least some flooded roads. Some bridges suffered damage, as well. The township with the greatest damage was Troy, with over $1 million in damages. One home in North Towanda had significant structural damage with a wall collapse due to the flash flooding.  Other homes only reported basement flooding or other minor damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked east across the northern Great Lakes during the morning and afternoon hours. The trailing cold front entered northwest Pennsylvania late that afternoon bringing a line of thunderstorms and high winds through the area. With the ground already saturated from the above normal precipitation, flooding also occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large trees. As a result of the downed trees, many power lines were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong southeast wind up the Delaware Bay and River combined with the already high astronomical tides associated with the full moon and fresh water runoff from heavy rain to produce severe tidal flooding along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries during the overnight high tide cycle on the 16th.||In Bucks County, homes along Minot Street in Bristol Township suffered basement flooding after a clapper failed. In Philadelphia, Columbus Boulevard and Delaware Avenue were completely shut down between Spring Garden Street and Washington Avenue. Cars were flooded by several feet of water. The Interstate 95 off ramp to Columbus Boulevard was closed. The tidal flooding waters came close, but did not reach the Waterfront Square Condominiums or Sugarhouse Casino on Delaware Avenue. In Delaware County, tidal flooding reached the John Heinz National Wildlife Refuge in Tinicum Township.||The high tide at Philadelphia Pier 12 reached 10.49 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on the 17th.  Severe tidal flooding begins at 10.2 feet above mean lower low water. This tied the all-time record high tide set during the November 25th nor'easter in 1950. The high tide at Marcus Hook (Delaware County) reached 9.80 feet above mean lower low water.||The strong southeast winds persisted and reached its peak as the high tide was working its way up the Delaware Bay and Delaware River. The surface winds did not shift to the west until about one hour before the high tide occurred in Philadelphia. Minor tidal flooding also occurred during the overnight high tide cycles on the 17th, 18th and 19th, but not on the magnitude of the 16th.

Several bands of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along two north-south axes of low level confluence across central Pennsylvania into upstate New York. The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and intense short-duration rainfall from the mid afternoon hours through the late evening. The combination of the heavy rain, above normal soil moisture and high stream-flows resulted in significant localized flash flooding in Sullivan County which lasted into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KAOO ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 52 knots or 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic area of low pressure and trailing frontal zone moved across southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening, producing intense lines of heavy showers and thunderstorms. Deep moisture and very strong wind fields ahead of the frontal system produced torrential rains, flash flooding and localized wind damage across the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was widespread across the area.  Some reports included flooding at Falling Spring Creek at Kennedy Street and 5th Avenue in Chambersburg as well as the   intersection of Hickory Run Road and Orestown Road.  A water rescue was reported in Southhampton Township.  Also in Chambersburg, Conococheague Creek was flooded at Commerce Street.  Roads were flooded and closed in Letterkenny and Lurgan Townships.  Iron Bridge Road was reported flooded at the Confluence of the West and East Branch of Antietam Creek near Waynesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms near a northward moving warm front produced wind damage in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos around midnight EDT on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees within Jim Thorpe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of April 3rd, an area of low pressure over Iowa moved northeast toward northern Michigan. A warm front extending east from the low across southern Ohio lifted north. By daybreak on the 4th, the warm front was located along the Lake Erie shoreline. The center of low pressure was located over northern Michigan, and the trailing cold front was located over Illinois. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the area between the warm and cold fronts. By nightfall, the showers and thunderstorms had diminished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several periods of very intense rain brought river flooding to the area.  Moderate river flooding occurred along the Yellow Breeches Creek bordering York and Cumberland Counties.  The West Branch of The Susquehanna at Muncy, as well as the mainstem Susquehanna at Bloomsburg also reported moderate flooding during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yellow Breeches Creek near Camp Hill crested above moderate flood stage. A number of homes along the entire length of Yellow Breeches Creek are affected by high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant severe weather and flash flood outbreak developed across northeast Pennsylvania as a storm system moved east from the Great Lakes.  Thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain developed and moved across the area during the overnight hours on the 28th. One supercell thunderstorm produced a tornado in Susquehanna county. Significant flash flooding also occurred across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damage was concentrated near market street to the west of the Huntsville Reservoir. Numerous White Pine and Eastern Hemlock trees were snapped with a few uprooted. The width of the damage swath was about 500 yards with the damage path length about 1 mile.

A garage was partially collapsed by the wind with 1 garage door completely blown in. A home sustained some damage to the roof as many shingles were blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front pushed northward across northeast Pennsylvania late in the afternoon on April 25th. A warm and relatively moist air mass covered the region, with showers and storms developing, especially in the unstable air south of the frontal boundary, where breaks in the cloud cover allowed temperatures to rise to near 70 degrees. Severe weather developed, and in addition to reports of severe wind damage and hail, areas of heavy rain caused significant flash flooding in several locations of northeast Pennsylvania.

Strong northwest winds affected the local Philadelphia area after a cold front moved through the region during the late morning on the 5th. This was in addition to isolated severe thunderstorms that moved through the area earlier that morning.  Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down tree limbs, some weak trees and wires. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 52 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 44 mph in London Grove (Chester County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania and mainly minor to moderate creek and  river flooding in Berks County and Southeastern Pennsylvania. The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches with the highest amounts along the interstate 95 corridor and the Poconos.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the tidal flooding along the Delaware River, the heavy rain caused poor drainage and creek flooding inland in Bucks County. The Neshaminy Creek had major flooding at Langhorne. In East Rockhill Township, Stone Hedge Road was closed because of creek flooding. In Plumstead Township, the intersection of Pennsylvania State Route 32 and Point Pleasant Pike was partially blocked by a mudslide. In Richland Township, flooding was reported along Pike and Ebert Roads. ||The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1054 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 935 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 12.14 feet at 5 a.m. EDT.  Precipitation event totals included 3.20 inches in Neshaminy Falls, 2.81 inches in Penndel, 2.60 inches in Richboro, 2.52 inches, 1.86 inches in Perkasie, 1.81 inches in Doylestown and 1.56 inches in Sellersville.

Coupled with the recent run of wet weather helped cause flash flooding in eastern parts of the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. The event precipitation total in Martins Creek in Lower Mount Bethel Township in Northampton County was 3.24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to high southeast winds affected Southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph with some isolated wind gusts to around 60 mph. The highest wind gusts occurred during the evening. The strong to high winds coupled with the heavy rain, knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires. The strong to high gradient winds were exacerbated further by isolated severe thunderstorms. About 25,000 homes and businesses lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania.  ||Peak wind gusts included 61 mph in Springtown (Bucks County), 58 mph in West Grove (Chester County) and 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and the Philadelphia International Airport. The strong to high winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system departing the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley. Winds eased after the occluded front moved through Pennsylvania late in the evening on the 16th.

The strong to high winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system departing the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley. Winds eased after the occluded front moved through Pennsylvania late in the evening on the 16th.
The deep counterclockwise circulation around a low pressure system that extended well upward into the atmosphere over the southern Appalachians helped cause a tornado in Philadelphia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with an estimated maximum wind speed of 75 mph briefly touched down in the northeast part of Philadelphia and damaged a couple of businesses and an apartment building in the area and caused two injuries. The tornado touched down near the intersection of Red Lion Road and Northeast Avenue. The roof of a car repair shop on Northeast Avenue caved in and injured two workers. One had to be hospitalized. An apartment complex near the garage was damaged and the Red Cross assisted four people in finding temporary quarters.  The roof of a beer distributor on Northeast Avenue collapsed. Torn shingles, signs and debris littered the grass around the distributor. A seafood restaurant on Bustelton Avenue then lost part of its roof.  The tornado then lifted. The path length was approximately two tenths of a mile and the maximum path width about 33 yards. The estimated maximum wind speed was 75 mph. This was the first tornado to touch down within Philadelphia since January 18, 1999.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold frontal boundary and low pressure system helped trigger scattered evening showers and thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania and an isolated severe thunderstorm in Bucks County during the late afternoon and evening on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Fogelsville (Upper Macungie Township).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted over Heidelberg Township. It did not touch down and no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved up from the Mississippi River Valley into Ohio and western Pennsylvania. The storm system had adequate moisture from the Gulf of Mexico, which increased rainfall rates to over an inch and a half per hour. This intense rainfall triggered several small rivers and streams to overflow their banks, and for low lying and poor drainage areas to flood. Rainfall amounts around one to two inches were observed. Saturated ground cover from recent rains supported complete runoff, so though relatively small rainfall amounts the flooding was still significant in areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall with rates over an inch per hour fell over portions of western Crawford County. Though rainfall amounts were generally one to two inches, the rain rate combined with saturated ground conditions resulted in rapid runoff. The runoff resulted in flooded basements and roads. Cars stalled along highway 6 near Maples Road as water depths were over hoods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front pushed northward across northeast Pennsylvania late in the afternoon on April 25th. A warm and relatively moist air mass covered the region, with showers and storms developing, especially in the unstable air south of the frontal boundary, where breaks in the cloud cover allowed temperatures to rise to near 70 degrees. Severe weather developed, and in addition to reports of severe wind damage and hail, areas of heavy rain caused significant flash flooding in several locations of northeast Pennsylvania.

A lee side trough helped trigger a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos during the evening of the 26th. Two roofs were damaged on the block and several trees and power lines were blown down. Albany Township was the last township to have power fully restored in Berks County on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm brought periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania. The initial round of mixed wintry precipitation (mainly snow and sleet) occurred during the early morning hours on the 1st, before tapering off to spotty freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening. A steadier and much heavier round of freezing rain mixed with sleet overspread the region from the late evening on the 1st and continued until the early morning on the 2nd. A minor snow and sleet accumulation of 2 to 4 inches was common near and to the north of Interstate 80. Significant ice accumulations between one quarter to three quarters of an inch were observed over nearly the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm produced periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain over the area. Snow and sleet accumulation was around 1 inch on the 1st, with 0.25 to 0.50 inches of ice on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved along a frontal boundary draped across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania and brought mixed freezing rain and snow to the Upper Ohio Valley. While most amounts where light, heavy snow fell across Forest and Venango counties with accumulations of 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving but intense winter storm moved through the region. This system brought moderate to heavy snow to the southern tier of central Pennsylvania, with accumulations ranging between 6 and 10 inches. The rapid strengthening of the surface low pressure system offshore the Mid-Atlantic coast produced thunder-snow and intense snowfall rates within narrow bands wrapping north-westward from the low center. An initial period of light snow occurred early in the morning, followed by a several hour lull in the precipitation. The bulk of the accumulations occurred between 1600 and 2200 EDT. The snow briefly mixed with sleet and freezing rain during the mid afternoon in the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged between 6 and 8 inches, most of which fell during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong to high northwest winds that occurred throughout the day caused blowout tides to occur along  tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries during the evening low tide cycle on the 19th. The low tide reached 2.00 feet below mean lower low water in Marcus Hook and 1.91 feet below mean lower low water at Philadelphia Pier 12. Blowout tides start at levels less than 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west winds followed a cold frontal passage during the afternoon and evening of the 14th in Eastern Pennsylvania. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and tore down weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 45 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, London Grove (Chester County) and Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 40 mph in Reading (Berks County). The strong winds were fueled by the pressure difference between the cold front's low pressure system that moved through New England and a high pressure system building east from the western Great Lakes. The strong winds slowly decreased overnight as the low pressure system moved farther away from Eastern Pennsylvania.

A protracted rain event from the early morning on the 6th through the early morning on the 8th culminated with heavy rain during the afternoon and evening of the 7th and caused poor drainage flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania. The rain changed to snow before ending in parts of Berks County and the Lehigh Valley as well as all of the Poconos, but most of the accumulations were confined to the higher terrain in the Poconos. The heavy rain also caused minor to locally moderate river and creek flooding in Berks County and Southeast Pennsylvania. Event precipitation totals averaged around two inches. The rain was caused by waves of low pressure that moved northeast along a slowly moving frontal boundary. The cold front was in central Pennsylvania on the morning of the 6th and eastern Pennsylvania on the morning of the 7th. The last wave of low pressure on this boundary, located over western North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 7th intensified rapidly as the 7th progress. It was during this intensification process that rain fell at its heaviest that afternoon and evening. The low pressure system itself passed through Delaware during the early evening of the 7th and was over the Monmouth County, New Jersey coastal waters at 10 p.m. EST on the 7th. It reached Long Island, New York at 1 a.m. EST on the 8th. As the low reached Long Island, the rain ended across Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne had moderate flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1134 p.m. EST on the 7th through 946 a.m. EST on the 8th. It crested at 10.61 feet at 330 a.m. EST on the 8th. Event precipitation totals included 2.61 inches in Penndel, 2.57 inches in Langhorne and Newtown, 2.42 inches in Doylestown and 2.15 inches in Pennsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying low pressure system caused strong northwest winds to occur across the higher terrain of the southern Poconos during the early morning on the 8th. Peak wind gusts were around 45 mph and downed tree limbs in the Poconos.  Peak wind gusts included 47 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). Other peak wind gusts in Eastern Pennsylvania included 43 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph in Reading (Berks County). The strong winds were caused by the increasing circulation around the same low pressure system that brought heavy rain and then snow to the Poconos. The low intensified from 1007 millibars over western North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 7th, to 996 millibars over Delaware at 7 p.m. EST on the 7th, to 989 millibars over Long Island at 1 a.m. EST on the 8th to 978 millibars over Eastport, Maine at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th. The 29 millibar drop in 24 hours qualified it for the meteorological definition of a bombing low pressure system and was the main reason for the strong winds. While the low continued to intensify (it was down to 963 millibars at the mouth of the Saint Lawrence River at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th), it moved far enough away from the Poconos so that strong winds subsided by the daytime on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system that developed along a warm front and moved east of the state on the 1st helped produce a light wintry mix in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties during the first half of the day on the 1st. Precipitation started as light snow, but changed over to sleet and freezing rain by the start of the morning commute. The freezing rain then changed to freezing drizzle. The freezing drizzle then changed to either plain drizzle late in the morning or ended. Snowfall accumulations were half an inch or less and ice accretions were just a couple of hundredths. Many roads were either pre-treated or had salt on them from past winter storms, so the number of accidents was relatively low.

Snow showers that preceded and accompanied a cold front dropped two to four inches of snow over the higher terrain of the Poconos from the evening of the 7th into sunrise on the 8th. Untreated roadways were hazardous. Specific accumulations included 4.2 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.3 inches in Pocono Summit and 2.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County).

Low pressure moving along a stalled front turned mixed precipitation to heavy wet snow the afternoon of the 21st across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia. The snow continued into the early morning of the 22nd. Snowfall rates up to 2 inches an hour produced storm totals of 6 to 10 inches across the region. Much of the snow fell during the evening rush hour from Steubenville, Ohio east to Pittsburgh and Greensburg, Pennsylvania bringing the evening commute to a standstill. Numerous cars were abandoned on roadways with some travelers spending nearly 8 hours to reach their destinations. Pockets of freezing rain were south of the area of heavy snow with more than one quarter of an inch of ice reported in Preston county West Virginia.

While the low continued to intensify (it was down to 963 millibars at the mouth of the Saint Lawrence River at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th), it moved far enough away from the Poconos so that strong winds subsided by the daytime on the 8th.

A large winter storm brought periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain to central Pennsylvania. The initial round of mixed wintry precipitation (mainly snow and sleet) occurred during the early morning hours on the 1st, before tapering off to spotty freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening. A steadier and much heavier round of freezing rain mixed with sleet overspread the region from the late evening on the 1st and continued until the early morning on the 2nd. A minor snow and sleet accumulation of 2 to 4 inches was common near and to the north of Interstate 80. Significant ice accumulations between one quarter to three quarters of an inch were observed over nearly the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm produced periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain over the area. Snow and sleet accumulation was around 1 inch on the 1st, with 0.25 to 0.50 inches of ice on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west winds followed a cold frontal passage during the afternoon and evening of the 14th in Eastern Pennsylvania. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and tore down weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 45 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, London Grove (Chester County) and Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and 40 mph in Reading (Berks County). The strong winds were fueled by the pressure difference between the cold front's low pressure system that moved through New England and a high pressure system building east from the western Great Lakes. The strong winds slowly decreased overnight as the low pressure system moved farther away from Eastern Pennsylvania.

A protracted rain event from the early morning on the 6th through the early morning on the 8th culminated with heavy rain during the afternoon and evening of the 7th and caused poor drainage flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania. The rain changed to snow before ending in parts of Berks County and the Lehigh Valley as well as all of the Poconos, but most of the accumulations were confined to the higher terrain in the Poconos. The heavy rain also caused minor to locally moderate river and creek flooding in Berks County and Southeast Pennsylvania. Event precipitation totals averaged around two inches. The rain was caused by waves of low pressure that moved northeast along a slowly moving frontal boundary. The cold front was in central Pennsylvania on the morning of the 6th and eastern Pennsylvania on the morning of the 7th. The last wave of low pressure on this boundary, located over western North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 7th intensified rapidly as the 7th progress. It was during this intensification process that rain fell at its heaviest that afternoon and evening. The low pressure system itself passed through Delaware during the early evening of the 7th and was over the Monmouth County, New Jersey coastal waters at 10 p.m. EST on the 7th. It reached Long Island, New York at 1 a.m. EST on the 8th. As the low reached Long Island, the rain ended across Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Manatawny Creek at Spangsville was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1233 a.m. EST through 612 a.m. EST on the 8th. It crested at 6.41 feet at 345 a.m. EST on the 8th. Event precipitation totals included 2.03 inches in Oley, 2.01 inches in Kutztown, 1.96 inches in Fleetwood, 1.80 inches in Oley and 1.66 inches in Reading.

A strong pressure gradient between a deep low pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and sprawling Great Lakes high pressure combined to produce damaging non-thunderstorm wind gusts. The strong wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph produced sporadic damage in Mifflin and Huntingdon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts estimated between 40 and 50 mph knocked down numerous tree limbs and caused localized power outages across the county. Minor roof damage was sustained to a few residences. A police car was struck by roof debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent cold northwest flow across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to redevelop over northwestern Pennsylvania during the evening hours of January 7th.  This activity intensified during the morning hours of the 8th.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile with snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour.  This activity finally diminished during the morning hours of the 9th.  Eight to twelve inches of snow fell on the northern half of Crawford County with lesser amounts further south.  A peak total of 14.5 inches was reported at Meadville.  Travel was hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent cold northwest flow across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to redevelop during the evening hours of January 7th.  This activity intensified during the early morning hours of the 8th.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile with snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour.  This activity finally diminished around midday on the 9th.  Eight to twelve inches of snow fell on the northern half of Crawford County with lesser amounts further south.  A peak total of 14.5 inches was reported at Meadville. Travel was hampered during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the central Great Lakes on January 6th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the day.  Cold northwest winds behind this low caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the late morning hours.  This activity intensified late in the day with periods of moderate to heavy snow reported during the evening hours.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile with snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour.  The snow shower activity finally diminished on the morning of the 7th.  Snowfall totals during this event ranged from 6 to 10 inches over much of inland Erie County.  A peak total of 14.8 inches was reported near Waterford with 10.7 inches at Franklin Center and 10.0 inches at Edinboro.|Travel during this event was difficult and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the central Great Lakes on January 6th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the day.  Cold northwest winds behind this low caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the late morning hours.  This activity intensified late in the day with periods of moderate to heavy snow reported during the evening hours.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile with snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour.  The snow shower activity finally diminished on the morning of the 7th.  Snowfall totals during this event ranged from 6 to 10 inches over much of inland Erie County.  A peak total of 14.8 inches was reported near Waterford with 10.7 inches at Franklin Center and 10.0 inches at Edinboro.

A steadier and much heavier round of freezing rain mixed with sleet overspread the region from the late evening on the 1st and continued until the early morning on the 2nd. A minor snow and sleet accumulation of 2 to 4 inches was common near and to the north of Interstate 80. Significant ice accumulations between one quarter to three quarters of an inch were observed over nearly the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm produced periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain over the area. Snow and sleet accumulation was around 1 inch on the 1st, with 0.25 to 0.50 inches of ice on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred across the Pocono Plateau during the evening of the 5th. Peak wind gusts were around 45 mph and included 46 mph at Mount Pocono (Monroe County).| |The strong winds were caused by the rapid intensification of the secondary low pressure system on the triple point (conjunction, of occluded, warm and cold fronts) of the frontal system that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania at the start of the evening of the 5th. The low pressure system deepened from 994 millibars to 980 millibars from 7 p.m. EST on the 5th to 1 a.m. EST on the 6th as it moved from northwest New Jersey to just on the southwest Maine coast. The strong winds ceased as the deepening low pressure system moved farther away from Pennsylvania.

An area of low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 11th.  Snow associated with this low began during the afternoon hours of the 11th with periods of moderate to heavy snow reported from late afternoon into the evening hours.  The snow lessened for a time during the late evening but picked up again after midnight on the 12th as cold northerly winds behind the low began to interact with Lake Erie.  More moderate to heavy snow occurred during the morning hours of the 12th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the afternoon.  Snowfall totals across most of Erie County ranged from 8 to 14 inches.  A peak total of 17.0 inches was reported at Colt Station with 14.7 inches at Franklin Center.  Officially, 9.6 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport on the 11th and 12th.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event and caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was very difficult during this event.  Many school districts were either closed or delayed during this storm.  Many accidents were reported.

Snowfall totals across inland  Erie County ranged from 8 to 14 inches.  A peak total of 17.0 inches was reported at Colt Station with 14.7 inches at Franklin Center.  Travel was very difficult during this event.  Many school districts were either closed or delayed during this storm.

Peak wind gusts included 49 mph in Reading (Berks County), 48 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 45 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and London Grove (Chester County), 44 mph in Doylestown and 41 mph Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

A fast-moving but intense winter storm moved through the region. This system brought moderate to heavy snow to the southern tier of central Pennsylvania, with accumulations ranging between 6 and 10 inches. The rapid strengthening of the surface low pressure system offshore the Mid-Atlantic coast produced thunder-snow and intense snowfall rates within narrow bands wrapping north-westward from the low center. An initial period of light snow occurred early in the morning, followed by a several hour lull in the precipitation. The bulk of the accumulations occurred between 1600 and 2200 EDT. The snow briefly mixed with sleet and freezing rain during the mid afternoon in the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged between 6 and 10 inches, with the heaviest amounts over the southern half of the county. There was a brief period of sleet and freezing rain during the early afternoon. Thunder-snow was observed during the evening accompanied by intense snowfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system, intially analyzed in east Texas on January 31st moved northeast over the Ohio Valley to western Pennsylvania by Wednesday morning, Febraury 2nd. Initial precipitation ahead of the warm front began as light snow before changing over to sleet and then freezing rain early on Tuesday Feb 1st. A break in the mixed precipitation occurred during the day on Tuesday with light rain and drizzle occuring for Pittsburgh and areas south while the counties north of I-80 continued to have snow. Tuesday evening, as the low pressure system approached the area another round of heavy precipitation moved over the region with the counties north of I-80 changing from snow to freezing rain. To the south, precipitation remained rain. Ice accumulations on the 1st ranged from one quarter to more than one half inch over eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. The counties north of I-80 generally had snow amounts between 4 and 6 inches with Venango county measuring 6.0 inches of snow in 6 hours. The weight of the ice caused downed trees and power outages through Carroll, Columbiana, Tuscarawas and Mercer counties.

On Monday, March 21st warm frontal showers and thunderstorms moved through during the early morning hours. A cold front moved across the region later in the afternoon bringing another round of showers and isolated thunderstorms. During the afternoon, the better threat for severe storms was located south of I-70 with the best instability over central West Virginia and southeastern Ohio. With instability limited, and a capping inversion in place, most storms did not reach severe criteria.

A total of six tornadoes were confirmed over from the early evening on the 27th through the early morning hours of the 28th. This was the most significant tornado outbreak in central Pennsylvania since 2005. The last event with 6 confirmed tornadoes was September 2004 with Hurricane Ivan. The large scale weather pattern that contributed to this outbreak featured a fast-moving upper level impulse and attendant surface low pressure system that moved across the Great Lakes Region. An unseasonably warm and relatively moist air mass /surface temperatures in the 70s to low 80s and dewpoints in the upper 50s/ resided over central Pennsylvania in advance of this system and aided in strong destabilization. Moderate instability combined with strong vertical wind shear profiles promoted a convective environment favorable for organized thunderstorms with supercells and bowing line segments. Although large hail and damaging winds were the main threats, sufficient low-level wind shear was present to support isolated tornadoes. A tornado watch was issued around 1500 EDT on 27 April 2011. The tornado threat continued through the evening and into the overnight and early morning hours on 28 April 2011. By this time, thunderstorms had evolved into largely north-south oriented clusters or broken lines with embedded supercell structures. The storms finally exited the region by mid-morning on 28 April 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS in State College confirmed an EF1 tornado near Palmyra in Lebanon County. The brief tornado occurred between 0620 and 0623 AM EDT on 28 April 2011. The EF1 tornado first produced damage just west of South Lingle Avenue (SR 3017) near West Pajabon Drive and proceeded along a 2 mile path northeast of the Palmyra Shopping Center along East Main Street (U.S. 422). Most of the damage indicators surveyed were associated with 75 to 85 mph winds, but a collapsed top floor of a house on 621 E. Main Street along with significant damage to an automobile dealership across the street supported maximum winds near 90 mph. Therefore, the tornado was rated a low-end EF1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system, originating from the southern plains, moved towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday March 9th. A wintry mix of precipitation changed over to all rain as the warm front associated with this system pushed across the region on Wednesday morning. Periods of moderate to heavy rain continued through the passage of the upper level trough late Thursday. With much of the region already saturated from previous rainfall over the past 2 weeks many streams and creeks were already elevated. The additional 1 to 2 inches of rainfall resulted in my streams and creeks gong out of their banks throughout east central Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. As the low progressed to the east rain changed back to snow bringing 4 to 5 inches of heavy wet snow to east central Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania, mainly north of I-70. Higher amounts of 6 to 8 inches fell in in Columbiana, Mercer, Lawrence and Venango counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County official reports of a few roads closed due to flooding in Hopewell and Darlington townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved off the Virginia coast helped spread snow across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 8th. The snow lasted overnight on the 8th in the Poconos. Snowfall averaged 1 to 3 inches with the least amounts in the Lehigh Valley. Snow began on the 8th across southeast Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST and in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos between 9 a.m. EST and 1030 a.m. EST. Snow ended during the middle of the afternoon on the 8th, except in the Poconos where snow showers persisted overnight. Untreated roadways were treacherous, especially during the morning and again at night in the Poconos. ||Representative snowfall included 2.9 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County), 2.7 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.2 inches in Morrisville (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County) and Bensalem (Bucks County), 1.9 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 1.7 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 1.5 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 1.0 inch in Reading (Berks County) and Martins Creek (Northampton County) and 0.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. The snow was caused by a low pressure system that moved southeast from the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 7th, across the Carolinas overnight on the 7th and then turned to the northeast. The low passed just east of Virginia Beach, Virginia at 4 a.m. EST on the 8th, moved northeast and was just east of the Delaware coast at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th. It started merging with another low that was moving across Pennsylvania. The merged lows intensified rapidly, but also started to accelerate and by 7 p.m. EST on the 8th, the single low was already south of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

Snow fell through the Friday morning commute on the 7th across Eastern Pennsylvania. Accumulations averaged one to three inches. Untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous. Numerous accidents were reported in Chester County. Snow began falling around Midnight EST on the 7th in the Poconos and prior to sunrise and the start of the morning commute on the 7th throughout the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow ended between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST on the 7th across most of Eastern Pennsylvania. ||Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 3.2 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 3.1 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Chesterbrook (Chester County) and Nazareth (Northampton County), 2.8 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 2.6 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 2.5 inches in Media (Delaware County) and East Nantmeal (Chester County), 2.4 inches in Spring Mount (Montgomery County), 2.3 inches in Kutztown (Berks County), 2.2 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), Drexel Hill (Delaware County), Emmaus (Lehigh County) and Exton (Chester County), 1.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Fricks (Bucks County), Reading (Berks County), Roxborough (Philadelphia) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County) and 1.3 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport. The snow was caused by a cold front that moved through Delaware during the morning of the 7th. The snow was also assisted by a vigorous upper air disturbance.

A low pressure system moved northeast from Missouri towards the Ohio Valley in the afternoon on January 11th, bringing light to moderate snow to eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. As the low pressure system over the Ohio Valley moved eastward and weakened, another low pressure system intensified along the coast of Long Island, NY bringing additional upslope heavy snow to the higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Maryland  and the on the ridges of Fayette County in Pennsylvania on January 12th and 13th. 15-17 inches of snowfall were recorded in Terra Alta, Preston County and Davis, Tucker County over the 48 hour period and 11.2 was recorded in a 24 hour period in Fayette County.

An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st.  A warm front stretching east from this low caused snow to develop over northwestern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 20th.  The snow continued through the early morning hours of the 21st and then tapered off around daybreak.  Some light freezing drizzle was then reported for a couple of hours.  Some of the snow during the evening was heavy with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour and visibilities less than a quarter mile.  Snowfall totals across Erie County ranged from 8 to 12 inches with 6 to 8 inches in Crawford County.  A peak total of 13.0 inches was reported near Colt Station in Erie County with a peak of 7.8 inches in Crawford County near Canadohta Lake.  Officially, 11.5 inches of snow was measured at Erie International Airport on the 20th and 21st.  Strong northeast winds accompanied the snow initially.  Winds speeds died off quickly during the evening hours but some blowing and drifting continued through the night.  Difficult travel conditions were reported.  A few school districts not already closed for the President's Day Holiday delayed or canceled classes on the 21st.  Travel during this event was difficult.

A warm front stretching east from this low caused snow to develop over northwestern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 20th. The snow continued through the early morning hours of the 21st and then tapered off around daybreak. Some light freezing drizzle was then reported for a couple of hours. Some of the snow during the evening was heavy with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour and visibilities less than a quarter mile. Snowfall totals across Erie County ranged from 8 to 12 inches.  Officially, 11.5 inches of snow was measured at Erie International Airport on the 20th and 21st. Strong northeast winds accompanied the snow initially. Winds speeds died off quickly during the evening hours but some blowing and drifting continued through the night. Difficult travel conditions were reported. A few school districts not already closed for the President's Day Holiday delayed or canceled classes on the 21st. Travel during this event was difficult.

A low pressure system that moved off the Virginia coast helped spread snow across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 8th. The snow lasted overnight on the 8th in the Poconos. Snowfall averaged 1 to 3 inches with the least amounts in the Lehigh Valley. Snow began on the 8th across southeast Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST and in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos between 9 a.m. EST and 1030 a.m. EST. Snow ended during the middle of the afternoon on the 8th, except in the Poconos where snow showers persisted overnight. Untreated roadways were treacherous, especially during the morning and again at night in the Poconos. ||Representative snowfall included 2.9 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County), 2.7 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.2 inches in Morrisville (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County) and Bensalem (Bucks County), 1.9 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 1.7 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 1.5 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 1.0 inch in Reading (Berks County) and Martins Creek (Northampton County) and 0.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. The snow was caused by a low pressure system that moved southeast from the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 7th, across the Carolinas overnight on the 7th and then turned to the northeast. The low passed just east of Virginia Beach, Virginia at 4 a.m. EST on the 8th, moved northeast and was just east of the Delaware coast at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th. It started merging with another low that was moving across Pennsylvania. The merged lows intensified rapidly, but also started to accelerate and by 7 p.m. EST on the 8th, the single low was already south of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

A deepening low pressure system, originating from the southern plains, moved towards the western Great Lakes on Wednesday March 9th. A wintry mix of precipitation changed over to all rain as the warm front associated with this system pushed across the region on Wednesday morning. Periods of moderate to heavy rain continued through the passage of the upper level trough late Thursday. With much of the region already saturated from previous rainfall over the past 2 weeks many streams and creeks were already elevated. The additional 1 to 2 inches of rainfall resulted in my streams and creeks gong out of their banks throughout east central Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. As the low progressed to the east rain changed back to snow bringing 4 to 5 inches of heavy wet snow to east central Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania, mainly north of I-70. Higher amounts of 6 to 8 inches fell in in Columbiana, Mercer, Lawrence and Venango counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington county emergency manager reported a water rescue on Catfish Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked slowly east across the Ohio Valley on February 20th and 21st.  A warm front stretching east from this low caused snow to develop over northwestern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 20th.  The snow continued through the early morning hours of the 21st and then tapered off around daybreak.  Some light freezing drizzle was then reported for a couple of hours.  Some of the snow during the evening was heavy with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour and visibilities less than a quarter mile.  Snowfall totals across Erie County ranged from 8 to 12 inches with 6 to 8 inches in Crawford County.  A peak total of 13.0 inches was reported near Colt Station in Erie County with a peak of 7.8 inches in Crawford County near Canadohta Lake.  Officially, 11.5 inches of snow was measured at Erie International Airport on the 20th and 21st.  Strong northeast winds accompanied the snow initially.  Winds speeds died off quickly during the evening hours but some blowing and drifting continued through the night.  Difficult travel conditions were reported.  A few school districts not already closed for the President's Day Holiday delayed or canceled classes on the 21st.  Travel during this event was difficult.
